text: The surgeon begins the mobilization of the ligament of Treitz using robotic instruments, carefully manipulating the surrounding tissue to expose and free the ligament.
The surgeon liberates the fourth portion of the duodenum and the first portion of the jejunum from the retroperitoneal space, continuing the dissection process.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to perform dissection, which functions as both a bipolar artery device and scissors, carefully navigating around the superior mesenteric vein and artery.
The surgeon carefully mobilizes the jejunum from the retroperitoneal space, using precise dissection techniques to separate the tissue.
The surgeon prepares for the mobilization and coparization of the duodenum, aiming to remove and resect the segment as part of the specimen and distal margin.
The surgeon uses a GIA stapler with a tan load to perform a surgical action, likely involving tissue approximation or division.
The surgeon uses a Cravidian stapler to divide the jejunum, further performing division of the mesentery with the stapler, extending the division as high as possible in this portion.
The surgeon approaches the fourth portion of the duodenum, aiming to position it beneath the mesenteric vessels, ensuring careful manipulation to avoid compromising surrounding structures.
The surgeon divides the antrum, using surgical instruments to separate it from surrounding tissues.
The lesser sac is visibly opened, and the surgeon passes a vessel sealer behind the stomach, preparing for further dissection or sealing of vessels.
The surgeon creates a tunnel for the GIA stapler to divide the antrum, preparing the tissue for further division.
The surgeon carefully places the GIA stapler and uses it to divide the stomach, ensuring precise alignment and division after thorough evaluation.
The surgeon ensures that no surrounding tissue is inadvertently grasped by the GIA stapler before proceeding with the division of the antrum.
The GIA stapler is activated, visibly firing to divide the stomach tissue.
The GIA stapler is shown with three rows of staples on either side, and a cutting mechanism positioned between the rows, ready to divide the tissue.
The surgeon completes the division of the stomach using the second load of the GIA stapler, ensuring precise separation of the tissue.
The surgeon uses a purple load of the GIA stapler to continue the division of the stomach, ensuring precise placement and operation.
The surgeon uses a bipolar instrument to grasp the gastrodial artery, which is visibly branching off the common hepatic artery. Further mobilization of the hepatic artery is observed.
The surgeon performs a lymph node dissection along the hepatic artery, which is clipped at the gastrodial artery, extending the dissection proximally towards the celiac axis.
The surgeon performs dissection at the level of the hepatic duct, with visible manipulation of surrounding tissues.
The surgeon grasps the cystic duct, which is positioned at the entrance of the gallbladder into the hepatic duct, adjacent to the common bile duct.
The surgeon grasps the cystic duct to achieve retraction, enhancing visualization of the surrounding area, including the portal vein.
The portal vein is visible to the right of the spatula cautery, highlighted during the dissection.
The surgeon identifies the hepatic artery with a branch extending to the right lobe of the liver, specifically the right hepatic artery, visible on the right side of the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the lymph nodes and the portal vein as structures tethering the common hepatic duct and common bile duct.

Using robotic instruments, the surgeon slides a spatula underneath the junction of the common hepatic duct and cystic duct, utilizing the reticulating capability of the tools for precise manipulation.
The surgeon uses a bipolar instrument to divide the common hepatic duct, with a stent visible within the duct.
The surgeon uses a bipolar grasper to shield the portal vein from potential cautery injury during the procedure.
After completing the division of the common hepatic duct, the portal triad and its surrounding lymph nodes become clearly visible.
The robotic instruments are used to carefully and meticulously dissect the hepatic ducts and portal vein, which are closely involved with each other.
The surgeon begins the division of the pancreas, using surgical instruments to carefully separate the pancreatic tissue.
The surgeon exposes the superior mesenteric vein below the caudal border of the left segment of the liver, revealing the anatomical relationship between these structures.
The surgeon uses a suction device to shield the superior mesenteric vein while performing cautery on the pancreas, ensuring protection of the vein during the procedure.
The surgeon cauterizes the inferior pancreaticoduodenal artery using a bipolar device, effectively minimizing blood loss during the procedure.
The surgeon approaches the pancreatic duct, with a visible hint of the duct's structure appearing in the surgical field.
The surgeon further mobilizes and divides the pancreas, enhancing the visualization of the cut surface of the organ.
The surgeon approaches the cranial aspect of the pancreatic division, focusing on the area where the pancreatic duct is visible.
The surgeon is visualizing the superior pancreaticoduodenal artery, focusing on its anatomical location and relation to surrounding structures.
The surgeon achieves hemostasis at the cut surface of the pancreas, ensuring control of bleeding in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the transition point where the superior mesenteric vein becomes the portal vein.
The surgeon liberates the superior mesenteric vein from its lateral attachments to the pancreas, with the pancreas and uncinate process positioned more posteriorly.
The surgeon begins cocorizing the duodenum, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues to expose the next portion of the surgical field.
The surgeon positions the vessel sealer, preparing to reveal the superior mesenteric artery and vein beneath it.
The surgeon continues to divide the attachments of the duodenum, facilitating further mobilization from the third to the fourth portion of the duodenum.
The surgeon carefully manipulates the delicate structures of the duodenum, using hand and wrist rotation to maintain a cautious dissection plane.
The surgeon retracts the tissue, revealing a possible hint of the jejunum as dissection continues.
The surgeon carefully peaks underneath the root of the mesentery, revealing underlying structures as the dissection progresses.
The surgeon repositions cranially to re-identify the superior mesenteric vein, following it caudally to visually locate and harvest lymph nodes along its path.

The surgeon mobilizes tissue underneath the root of the mesentery, navigating between the portion below the transverse mesocolon and the portion above it, highlighting the complexity of the operation.
The surgeon utilizes an open approach, revealing the surgical field for further dissection and exploration of the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon passes the jejunum blindly underneath the superior mesenteric vein and superior mesenteric artery, navigating through the complex anatomical space beneath the root of the mesentery.
The surgeon visually identifies the structure, securely grasping it and pulling it towards them with precision.
The surgeon retracts the jejunum from underneath the superior mesenteric vein (SMV) and superior mesenteric artery (SMA), focusing on the upper aspect of the abdomen.
The surgeon rotates the mesentery and releases all vascular attachments under direct visualization, ensuring clear identification and separation of these structures.
The surgeon identifies and takes small blood vessels that were previously difficult to visualize, using direct visualization techniques in the upper aspect of the abdomen.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to immobilize the tissue at a high position, facilitating the surgical process.
The surgeon uses a GIA stapler with gray vascular loads to capture and secure the small blood vessels leading directly into the uncinate process.
The surgeon meticulously harvests lymph nodes along the lateral aspect of the superior mesenteric artery, which is clearly visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon observes the artery visibly pulsating in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies a small arterial branch adjacent to the superior mesenteric artery and prepares to divide it using a GIA stapler.
The view shows the superior metatarsal artery extending cranially, with visible pulsation indicating blood flow.
The surgical field extends to the level of the porta hepatis, with attention to the vascular supply to achieve the widest possible radial margin.
The surgeon demonstrates the posterior fixation of the pancreas, showing the anatomical positioning and attachment of the pancreas in relation to surrounding structures.
The surgeon approaches the duct of the pancreas to perform an anastomosis with the mucosa of the jejunum, indicating the completion of the previous step involving the jejunum.
The surgeon places the posterior stitch first during the reconstruction phase, ensuring optimal positioning for the anastomosis between the pancreas duct and jejunum mucosa.
The pancreatic duct appears decompressed with a stent, measuring approximately three millimeters in diameter. A suture is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon ties the sutures with precise tension and cuts them, ensuring secure closure of the tissue.
The surgeon uses a robotic instrument to grasp and manipulate the needle at various angles, demonstrating precise control over its positioning within the surgical field.
The surgeon places sutures at the ten o'clock position on the pancreas and then, after cleaning the camera, takes a suture bite on the jejunum.
The surgeon withdraws the suture to the appropriate length and ties it securely, completing the suturing process.
The surgeon places a suture at the two o'clock position, taking a healthy bite on the jejunum without tying it to avoid obscuring the placement of the next stitch.
The surgeon ties the previously placed suture, securing it in position on the jejunum.
The surgeon places the final suture at the twelve o'clock position on the jejunum and ties it securely, completing the suturing process.
The surgeon places the last suture of the pancreaticojejunostomy anastomosis, preparing to pass the V-lock suture that was initially started.
The surgeon uses the same suture to create the top row, relieving tension and further buttressing the pancreatic anastomosis.
The surgeon uses a B-lock suture, visible with small barbs designed to prevent slipping, eliminating the need for a traditional knot at the end.
The pancreatic anastomosis is completed, and the surgical focus shifts to the hepaticojejunostomy, which involves the bile duct and is visually facilitated by its larger size.
A drain is placed near the hepatic duct, which is visible in the surgical field.
The video shows a comparison between the bile duct and the pancreatic duct, highlighting the significantly larger size of the bile duct.
The surgeon creates a small incision in the jejunum to perform a single-layer interrupted anastomosis.
The surgeon begins the biliary anastomosis at the five o'clock position, methodically placing six to eight sutures around the circumference of the duct.
The surgeon inspects the anastomosis site, identifying areas where adjunctive sutures need to be placed to ensure proper connection and stability.
